rulings.Case Title: IN RE: SAN FRANCISCO HOUSING AUTHORITY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner San Francisco Housing Authority'S Hearing Of Verified Petition For An Order To Perpetuate Testimony
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 30, 2014, Line 7 - Petitioner San Francisco Housing Authority'S Hearing Of Verified Petition For An Order To Perpetuate Testimony. Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear to discuss status of claim process and locating witnesses and how the respective interests of the parties can be addressed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rkane1089@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JANET FIORE VS. KEVIN WALLACE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 30, 2014, Line 14, DEFENDANTS KEVIN WALLACE, WALLACE REMODELING INC.'S MOTION TO STRIKE PORTIONS OF THE COMPLAINT. Defendants Wallace and Wallace Remodeling, Inc.'s Motion to Strike Portions of the Complaint is denied. Defendants failed to comply with California Rule of Court 3.1322 requiring a notice of motion to strike a portion of the pleading to quote the full portion sought to be stricken except where the motion is to strike an entire paragraph or a complete cause of action. Defendants' Motion sought both selected portions and entire paragraphs of the Complaint be struck. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: JANET FIORE VS. KEVIN WALLACE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 30, 2014, Line 14, DEFENDANT KEVIN WALLACE, WALLACE REMODELING INC.'s DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. The Demurrer to the Complaint is overruled. As to Causes of Action One, Three, Five, and Eleven, the Complaint states facts sufficient to constitute causes of action against Defendants Kevin Wallace and Wallace Remodeling, Inc. C.C.P. § 430.10(e). The Complaint adequately alleges the statutory elements of these causes of action. The Complaint also adequately pleads who, what, when, and how the alleged misrepresentations were made. The Demurrer to the Sixth and Seventh Causes of Action is overruled because the Complaint sufficiently alleges facts sufficient to constitute causes of action for Professional Negligence and Negligent/Defective Construction. Defendants Kevin Wallace and Wallace Remodeling, Inc.'s Demurrers to the Complaint on the ground of uncertainty are overruled. C.C.P. § 430.10(f). Defendants have not addressed in their memorandum of points and authorities how the First, Third, Fifth, and Eleventh Causes of Action are uncertain. The Sixth Cause of Action alleges sufficient facts to constitute a cause of action and is not uncertain. The title of the Seventh Cause of Action does not make that cause of action uncertain. See Williams v. Beechnut Nutrition Corp. (1986) 185 Cal.App.3d 135, 139, f.n. 2. The Eleventh Cause of Action states a cause of action and a demurrer cannot attack the amount of damages requested in a complaint. See Venice Town Council, Inc. v. City of Los Angeles (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 1547, 1561-62. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: YOUNG CHI VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 30, 2014, Line 16, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Sustained with 10 days leave to amend. Although a writ of administrative mandamus is the only procedural mechanism available to challenge the Police Commission's termination decision, the complaint also alleges that the California Public Safety Officers' Procedural Bill of Rights Act ("POBRA") was violated because Plaintiff was not provided with the opportunity for an administrative appeal. Cal. Gov. code sec. 3309.5 provides that "[t]he superior court shall have initial jurisdiction over any proceeding brought by any public safety officer against any public safety department for alleged violations of this chapter." Plaintiff is given leave to amend to specifically identify the provision of POBRA that provides for an administrative appeal of a police commission's termination decision. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)
